parsing_res_list.block_content: of soil $\mathrm{CH}_{4}$ uptake at two initial $\mathrm{CH}_{4}$  inhibition of $\mathrm{CH_{4}}$ avoid nitrification of added $ NH_4^{+}$ in the soils and the effects of the associated counter-ions $(\mathrm{e.g.,~S O_{4}^{2-},~C l^{-1}})$ 1,we used urea-N together with a nitrification inhibitor,dicyandiamide, which can build up high levels of $\mathrm{NH_4^+-N}$ accumulation with little $\mathrm{NO}_{3}^{-}-\bar{\mathrm{N}}$ . This  allowed  us  to compare the inhibition by $\mathrm{NH_4^+-N}$ with that by $\mathrm{NO}_{3}^{-}-\mathrm{N}$ of the soil $\mathrm{CH_{4}}$ uptake. We also studied the effects of dicyandiamide addition alone on the $\mathrm{CH}_{4}$ oxidation in surface forest soils.

After removal of the above-ground plant debrs soil samples were taken at Numata farm (36°36'N, 139°00'E,altitude 780–810 m), Chiba University, from a typical Japanese cedar forest (Cryptomeria japonica, 40 years old) and its adjacent orchard dominated by apple trees (Malus pumila, 15 years old) in November 2002. The typical forest land lies 200 m above the orchard. The surface slopes of both sites are within the range of 7–12°.Three different soil layers, based on the soils'attributes,were sampled from 0 to 26, 26 to 51, and 51 to 75 cm depth under the coniferous forest, and from 0 to 9, 9 to 23,and 23 to 51 cm under the orchard, respectively. Fresh soils, classified as andisol (FAO soil classification), were sieved (2 mm) to remove small stones and roots in the laboratory and stored in the dark below $5^{\circ}C$ prior to incubation.$\mathrm{NH_4^+-N}$ and $\mathrm{NO}_{3}^{-}\mathrm{-N}$ in the soils were extracted with 1 M KCl solution. The slurry was shaken for 30 min before filtering. The $\mathrm{NH_4^+-N\ and\ NO_3^--N}$ in the soil extract was  measured  colorimetrically via  the nitroprusside and hydrazine-reduction methods, respectively (Kim 1995). Soil water content was measured by drying the soil at 105C for 24 h. The total soil C and N contents were measured  using a CN analyzer (MT-700

with an Auto Samper MTa-0;Yanaco, Japan), via ilb pH meter. The main soil characteristics are summarized in Table 1. Some surface forest soils (0−5 cm depth) from the same farm were also taken to study the effect  of dicyandiamide addition alone on $\mathrm{CH}_{4}$ uptake in soil;their main attributes were measured as mentioned earlier and are shown in Table 2.
Soil $\mathrm{CH}_{4}$ uptake capacity at two initial $\mathrm{CH_{4}}$ 1concentrations

The soil moisture was adjusted to approximately 45% after $5.0-g$  samples of fresh soil (in triplicate) were added to 100-ml serum bottles which were sealed with butyl rubber stoppers. Some wet soil samples from the lower mineral layers were allowed a period of air-drying to standardize their moisture content (approximately 45%) before the incubation. Through injecting a series of standard $\mathrm{CH_{4}}$ 1volumes with a concentration of 996.1 μl $\mathrm{l^{-1}\_CH_{4}^{-}}$ (Takachiho Industrial, Japan) into each bottle, the headspace $\mathrm{CH}_{4}$ concentrations prior to the incubation were approximately 2.4 and 12.6 l $ l^{-1}\ CH_{4}$ ., respectively.Finally the incubation was carried out at $25^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ in the dark.At regular intervals 0.5-ml headspace gases were taken and immediately injected into a gas chromatograph (GC)for $\mathrm{CH}_{4}$  measurement. Control bottles containing no soil were included to show that the rubber stoppers produced no measurable $\mathrm{CH}_{4}$ under any of the experimental conditions.
Effect of different N sources on soil $\mathrm{CH}_{4}$ uptake at depths
To study the N inhibition of the soil $\mathrm{CH_{4}}$ uptake, aqueous solutions of urea-N or $\mathrm{KNO_{3}-N}$ were sprayed on the soil and distributed throughout the sample by gentle mixing;
